context: For Ezra had prepared his heart to seek the law of the Lord.
point_summary: Ezra's preparation of heart to seek the law of the Lord, followed by doing it and teaching it, illustrates both purposes for learning: personal application and the dissemination of truth to others.

context: Ezra had prepared his heart to seek the law of the Lord... and to teach in Israel statutes and judgments
point_summary: Ezra's preparation of heart to seek the law of the Lord illustrates both purposes for learning—to do it (application) and to teach it (dissemination)—as the structural backbone of the sermon's thesis.

context: Ezra prepared his heart to seek the law of the Lord, and to do it, and to teach in Israel statutes and judgments.
point_summary: Ezra's threefold pattern—preparing the heart, doing the law, and teaching it to others—is presented as the model and memory verse for the sermon's two purposes of learning: personal application and outward dissemination.

context: Ezra, the Bible tells us, was a ready scribe, and he prepared his heart to seek the law of the Lord, and to do it, and to teach in Israel statutes and judgments
point_summary: Ezra's threefold commitment—to seek the law, to do it, and to teach it—illustrates that learning Scripture carries the dual purpose of personal application and the dissemination of truth to others.

context: Ezra 8, beginning in verse number 21
point_summary: Ezra 8:21 is introduced as an Old Testament illustration of God's ability to answer prayer, with the returning exiles' journey to Jerusalem framing that theme.

context: because we had spoken unto the king saying, the hand of our God is upon all them for good that seek him, but his power and his wrath is against all them that forsake him.
point_summary: Ezra's shame in asking the king for military escort arose from his prior testimony that God protects all who seek Him, making prayer and fasting the only consistent response.

context: so we fasted and besought our God for this. That's prayer. Prayer and fasting. And I want you to notice at the end of verse 23, it says, and he was entreated of us.
point_summary: Ezra's public declaration that God protects those who seek Him made it a matter of integrity to fast and pray rather than seek military escort, and God answered that prayer.

context: we find here in Ezra chapter 8 an illustration how that people, that they seek the face of God
point_summary: Ezra 8 illustrates that when God's people refuse available human resources and instead fast and pray in a dangerous situation, God personally delivers them and provides every protection needed.

context: Ezra 7 and verse number 10 if you want to jot that reference down but Ezra says in that passage of Scripture
point_summary: Ezra's prior preparation—seeking, doing, then teaching the law—is held up as the model for how believers must ready themselves before gospel opportunities arise.

context: We see specifically what it says in Ezra chapter four
point_summary: Ezra 4:1 is cited as background context showing that when adversaries opposed the returned exiles, Israel abandoned temple construction for fourteen years—a failure of perseverance that God did not approve.

context: you can go back to reference it back to Ezra chapter four in verse 24 to see what all was taking place there
point_summary: Ezra 4:24 provides the backstory for Haggai's rebuke: fourteen years of dormant temple-building, which God was not pleased with, set the stage for the call to resume the work.

context: I preached a message a few weeks ago on a Wednesday evening from the book of Nehemiah and excuse me from the book of Ezra and as we look at that passage of Scripture and we deal with the subject matter as to what we were dealing with we find that there Ezra understood that the hand of his God was upon him
point_summary: Ezra's confidence in declaring God's greatness to the king was tested when he faced a dangerous journey to Jerusalem, illustrating that living as salt and light requires trusting that God's hand is upon his people.

context: The Bible says in Ezra 7 and verse number 10 that he prepared his heart to seek and to do and to teach in Israel statutes and judgments
point_summary: Ezra's deliberate preparation of heart to seek, do, and teach illustrates that readiness is necessary, but must be subordinated to dependence on the Holy Spirit rather than trusted as sufficient in itself.

context: Ezra in Ezra chapter seven in verse number six as a ready scribe.
point_summary: Ezra is called a "ready scribe" in 7:6 because, as verse 10 explains, he had prepared his heart to seek, do, and teach God's law — making his readiness a matter of inward preparation before outward service.

context: The Bible says, for Ezra had prepared his heart to seek the law of the Lord and to do it and to teach in Israel statutes and judgments.
point_summary: Ezra is called a "ready scribe" because he had prepared his heart to seek, do, and teach God's law — a preparation God himself recognized and rewarded with unique opportunity.

context: blessed be the Lord God of our fathers which hath put such a thing as this where in the king's heart to beautify the house of the Lord
point_summary: God's sovereign ability to move the hearts of pagan kings toward His purposes is celebrated in Ezra 7:27, countering the objection that He cannot work through ungodly leaders.

context: Ezra 8, beginning in verse number 21
point_summary: Ezra 8:21 is introduced as an Old Testament illustration of God's ability to answer prayer, with the returning exiles' journey to Jerusalem framing that point.

context: because we had spoken unto the king saying, the hand of our God is upon all them for good that seek him, but his power and his wrath is against all them that forsake him.
point_summary: Ezra's shame at requesting a military escort arose from his prior testimony to the king that God protects all who seek Him, making prayer and fasting the only consistent response.

context: So we fasted and besought our God for this. That's prayer. Prayer and fasting. And I want you to notice the end of verse 23, it says, and he was entreated of us.
point_summary: Ezra's company fasted and prayed rather than requesting military escort because their prior testimony to the king—that God protects those who seek Him—made trusting God the only consistent response.

context: But we find here in Ezra chapter 8 an illustration how that people, that they seek the face of God and in the midst of a serious situation, they determine that they're not going to rely on the resources that might be available to them through some other means, but they're going to trust the Lord.
point_summary: Ezra 8 illustrates that when God's people refuse available human resources and instead fast and pray in a dangerous situation, God personally delivers them and provides traveling mercies.

context: The Bible says in verse number 1 of Ezra 9, Now when these things were done, the princes came to me, saying, The people of Israel and the priests and the Levites have not separated themselves from the people of the lands, doing according to their abominations, even of the Canaanites, the Hittites, the Perizzites, the Jebusites, the Ammonites, the Moabites, the Egyptians, and the Amorites.
point_summary: Ezra 9:1 introduces a revival passage in which Israel's failure to separate from surrounding nations — even among its leaders — provokes Ezra's grief-stricken response as the necessary prelude to national repentance.

context: For they have taken up their daughters for themselves and for their sons, so that the holy seed have mingled themselves with the people of those lands. Yea, the hand of the princes and rulers hath been chief in this trespass.
point_summary: Israel's failure to remain separate from surrounding peoples — with rulers leading the transgression — is presented as the corporate sin requiring the kind of broken, shame-filled repentance Ezra models.

context: And when I heard this thing, I rent my garment and my mantle, and plucked off the hair of my head and of my beard, and sat down a stone-eyed.
point_summary: Ezra's tearing of his garments and sitting astonished models the grief-stricken, repentant response the sermon calls Americans to adopt in the face of national sin.

context: Then were assembled unto me every one that trembled at the words of the God of Israel, because of the transgression of those that had been carried away, and I sat a stone-eyed until the evening sacrifice.
point_summary: Those who tremble at God's word assemble in corporate grief over national sin, modeling the repentance the sermon calls America to embrace.

context: And at the evening sacrifice I arose up from my heaviness, and having rent my garment and my mantle, I fell upon my knees, and spread out my hands unto the Lord my God,
point_summary: Ezra's posture of prostrate, spread-handed prayer accompanied by shame and confession models the kind of corporate repentance the sermon calls America to embrace in the face of divine judgment.

context: O my God, I am ashamed and blush to lift up my face to thee, my God, for our iniquities are increased over our head, and our trespass is grown up unto the heavens.
point_summary: Ezra's confession of overwhelming national sin—shame so deep he could not lift his face to God—is held up as the model of broken-hearted intercession that genuine revival and averted judgment require.

context: lifting up our face to the Lord and saying, our iniquities are increased over our head.
point_summary: Ezra's posture of broken intercession—confessing national iniquity with heaviness rather than hoping for judgment—is held up as the model response to awareness of a nation's sin.

context: since the days of our fathers have we been in great trespass unto this day. And for our iniquities have we, our kings and our priests, been delivered into the hand of the kings of the lands, to the sword, to captivity, to a spoil, to confusion of face as it is this day.
point_summary: Ezra's confession that accumulated national iniquity brings divine judgment serves as a model for how believers today should respond to their own nation's sin—with brokenness rather than indifference.

context: Now for a little space, grace hath been showed from the Lord our God to leave us a remnant
point_summary: God's grace in preserving a remnant amid national sin—pictured in Ezra's broken, intercessory response—is held up as the model for how believers should respond to their own nation's moral decline.

context: to escape and to give us a nail in His holy place, that our God may lighten our eyes and give us a little reviving in our bondage.
point_summary: Ezra's posture of broken intercession over national sin—lifting his face to God in confession that "our iniquities are increased over our head"—is held up as the model response for believers witnessing a nation ripe for judgment.

context: The Bible says in Ezra 7, in verse number 1, now after these things, in the reign of Artaxerxes, king of Persia, Ezra, the son of Sariah, the son of Azariah, the son of Hilkiah, the son of Shalom, the son of Zadok, the son of Ahitab, the son of Amoriah, the son of Azariah, the son of Merioth, the son of Zerahiah, the son of Uzziah, the son of Bukkiah, the son of Abishua, the son of Phinehas, the son of Eleazar, the son of Aaron, the chief priest.
point_summary: Before Ezra ever stood to teach the people in Nehemiah 8, the opening of Ezra 7 establishes the prior preparation—genealogical, historical, and personal—that made him a ready scribe.

context: This Ezra, notice in verse number 6, went up from Babylon and he was a ready scribe in the law of Moses, which the Lord God of Israel had given, and the king granted him all his requests according to the hand of the Lord his God upon him.
point_summary: Ezra's description as a "ready scribe" in verse 6 is presented as the fruit of prior preparation, setting up the sermon's focus on what God does in a man before public ministry begins.

context: Verse number 7, and there went up some of the children of Israel, and of the priests and the Levites and the singers and the porters and the Nethanims unto Jerusalem in the seventh year of Artaxerxes the king.
point_summary: Ezra 7:7 is read as part of the narrative context establishing the journey to Jerusalem, setting the stage for the sermon's focus on Ezra's prior preparation before his public ministry.

context: For upon the first day of the first month began he to go up from Babylon, and on the first day of the fifth month came he to Jerusalem according to the good hand of his God upon him.
point_summary: Ezra 7:6–9 frames the circumstances of Ezra's ministry, establishing that his journey from Babylon to Jerusalem was accomplished under the good hand of God as part of the promised remnant return foretold by Jeremiah.

context: For Ezra had prepared his heart to seek the law of the Lord and to do it and to teach in Israel statutes and judgments.
point_summary: Ezra 7:10 presents a three-part sequence—seeking, doing, and teaching the law—as the defining preparation of Ezra's heart that explains his readiness and effectiveness as a scribe.

context: His genealogy is described in verses 1 through 5, and we find that it's traced back to Aaron the chief priest.
point_summary: Ezra's genealogy in 7:1–5, traced back to Aaron the chief priest, establishes his priestly credentials as the foundation for understanding his authority and role in the promised return of the remnant.

context: And we begin to get into verses 6 through 9, and we begin to understand kind of some of the circumstances in which Ezra was serving the Lord.
point_summary: Ezra's success in obtaining the king's full favor is attributed directly to the hand of the Lord his God upon him, not to his own merit or political standing.

context: Ezra is now returning, it says in verse 8 that he came to Jerusalem in the fifth month
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: we find in chapter number 7 when Ezra is now returning, it says in verse 8 that he came to Jerusalem in the fifth month, and you find that God's hand was upon him, the good hand of his God upon him. We see that in verse number 6
point_summary: The repeated notice that "the good hand of his God was upon him" (vv. 6, 9) frames Ezra's return to Jerusalem as operating under evident divine favor, setting the stage for the exposition of his prepared heart in verse 10.

context: you find that God's hand was upon him, the good hand of his God upon him. We see that in verse number 6, and again in verse number 9
point_summary: Ezra 7:9's repeated phrase "the good hand of his God upon him" frames the surrounding context of Ezra's arrival in Jerusalem as evidence of divine favor before the sermon pivots to the heart preparation of verse 10.

context: but I want you to focus upon verse number 10
point_summary: Ezra 7:10 is broken down phrase by phrase to show that Ezra's effectiveness began with a prepared heart oriented toward seeking, doing, and teaching God's law.

context: Notice as we continue through this verse Ezra 7 and verse number 10
point_summary: Ezra 7:10 is shown to demand not merely study of God's Word but active obedience—preparing the heart to seek the law is inseparable from doing it.

context: Ezra had prepared his heart to seek the law of the Lord and to do it notice the end of Ezra 7 in verse number 10 the Bible says this and to teach in Israel statutes and judgments
point_summary: Ezra's preparation to seek, do, and teach the law reveals that Scripture's truth is not merely personal but carries an obligation to pass it on to others.

context: ...Ezra had already prepared...to teach in Israel statutes and judgments...
point_summary: Ezra's preparation to seek, do, and teach God's law preceded any opportunity to teach, demonstrating that faithful instruction of others flows from prior personal readiness in Scripture.

context: ...the Bible says and thou Ezra after the wisdom of thy God...
point_summary: Ezra's prior preparation to seek, do, and teach God's law made him ready when the divine commission came to appoint judges and instruct those who did not yet know God's laws.

context: now remember the Bible told us back in Ezra chapter 7 in verse number 6 that he was a ready scribe in the law of Moses
point_summary: Ezra's readiness as a scribe in the law of Moses (7:6) directly equipped him to fulfill the teaching commission of Nehemiah 8, where the people called on him to bring the book of the law.

context: ...God gave us to the character of this man Ezra back in Ezra chapter number 7...
point_summary: Nehemiah 8's model of reading Scripture distinctly, giving the sense, and causing understanding is traced back to the character and preparation of Ezra established in Ezra 7.

context: ...Notice in verse number 12, and all the people went their way to eat and to drink...
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Ezra, he was a ready scribe, and as we looked at the truth about his life and we understand the things that he did, we mentioned from Ezra 7 and verse number 10 that he prepared his heart to seek the law of the Lord.
point_summary: Ezra's life followed a deliberate three-part pattern: he prepared his heart to seek God's law, purposed to obey it, and then planned to teach it to others in Israel.

context: In Ezra eight, in verse 31, the Bible says this, 'Then we departed from the river of Ahava on the twelfth day of the first month to go
point_summary: God's protective hand upon Ezra's company during their journey to Jerusalem serves as the sermon's governing theme, drawn directly from the phrase "the hand of our God was upon us" in verse 31.

context: blessed be the Lord God of our fathers, which hath put such a thing as this in the king's heart to beautify the house of the Lord, which is in Jerusalem
point_summary: Ezra's shame over requesting a military escort is grounded in his prior testimony to the king that God's hand protects all who trust Him, making such a request a contradiction of his own declared faith.

context: and whatsoever more shall be needful for the house of thy God, which thou shalt have occasion to bestow, bestow it out of the king's treasure house
point_summary: Ezra's shame over requesting a military escort is grounded in his prior testimony to the king that God's hand protects all who trust Him, making such a request a contradiction of his own declared faith.

context: and whosoever will not do the law of thy God and the law of the king of judgment be executed speedily upon him, whether it be unto death or to banishment or to confiscation of goods or to imprisonment
point_summary: Ezra's shame in requesting a military escort arose directly from his prior testimony to the king that God's hand protects all who seek Him—making such a request a contradiction of his own declared faith.

context: then I proclaimed a fast there at the river of Ahava that we might afflict ourselves before our God to seek of him a right way for us and for our little ones and for all our substance.
point_summary: Fasting and prayer, not military escort, was Ezra's chosen means of seeking God's protection, demonstrating that proclaimed trust in God must be lived out in practice, not merely declared.

context: so we fasted and besought our God for this and he was entreated of us.
point_summary: Fasting and prayer—not military escort—was the means by which Ezra's company sought God's protection, demonstrating that proclaimed trust in God must be lived out, not merely declared.

context: In this evening, if we look at Ezra in Ezra 8, we would have to conclude from his behavior that he believed God.
point_summary: Ezra's willingness to forgo a military escort, trusting God's protection on the journey, demonstrates that genuine belief in God's provision must be confirmed by corresponding behavior.

context: I referenced last week on Wednesday evening when we dealt with that subject of prayer and developing our attitude towards prayer, and we looked at a text of Scripture in Ezra chapter number 8 where Ezra was ashamed to ask for a group of soldiers to come and to help them along the way against the enemy.
point_summary: Ezra's refusal to request a military escort—because he had already declared God's greatness to the king—illustrates that publicly professing God's sufficiency obligates believers to trust Him visibly in crisis.

context: he prepared his heart to seek the law of the Lord and to do it, right? And to teach in Israel statutes and judgments. Ezra 7 and verse number 10
point_summary: Ezra 7:10 is cited as a parallel to Moses's obedience, illustrating the scriptural pattern of preparing one's heart to seek, do, and teach God's law.

context: he was a ready scribe in the law of Moses
point_summary: Ezra's designation as a "ready scribe" is traced to heart preparation—seeking, doing, and then teaching the law—offered as the model for why Philip could open his mouth and preach Christ without hesitation.

context: for Ezra had prepared his heart to seek the law of the Lord and to do it and to teach in Israel statutes and judgments
point_summary: Ezra 7:10 is cited to show that readiness to preach Christ—like Philip's—flows from a heart prepared first to seek Scripture, then to live it personally, and only then to teach it to others.

context: Ezra in Ezra chapter number seven, that he was a prepared scribe, but he was a, excuse me, a ready scribe, and he prepared his heart to seek the law of the Lord in Israel and to do it, excuse me, and to teach in Israel statutes and judgments
point_summary: Ezra's example establishes that faithful teaching requires prior personal study, obedience, and practice—one must first seek, do, and then teach.

context: He had prepared his heart to seek the law of the Lord and to do it and to teach in Israel statutes and judgment according to Ezra 7 and verse 10
point_summary: Ezra's readiness for revival is grounded in a deliberate, ordered preparation—setting his heart to seek God's law, do it, and then teach it.

context: turn over to Ezra, if you will, in chapter number eight
point_summary: Ezra 8:21 is cited to show that fasting is an act of self-affliction and humility by which God's people earnestly seek His direction, not to earn favor but to demonstrate dependence on Him.

context: the Bible says, then I proclaimed a fast there at the river of Ahava that we might afflict ourselves before our God, notice what it says, to seek of him a right way for us and for our little ones and for all our substance
point_summary: Fasting in Ezra 8:21 is presented as the means by which Ezra and the people humbled themselves before God to seek His direction, provision, and protection for their journey to Jerusalem.

context: in verse 22, it says for I was ashamed to require of the king a band of soldiers and horsemen to help us against the enemy in the way
point_summary: Ezra's shame at asking the king for military protection—after having declared God's sufficiency to that same king—illustrates how fasting and humility flow from a genuine, consistent trust in God's power and provision.

context: I'm ashamed, is what he says in verse 22, to require of the king a band of soldiers
point_summary: Ezra's shame at asking the king for military escort arose directly from his prior testimony that God protects all who seek Him, making inconsistency between his words and his actions the very logic driving him to fast and seek God instead.

context: In verse 23, it says so we fasted and besought our God for this, and notice what it says, he was entreated of us
point_summary: Fasting accompanied by earnest prayer demonstrates a seriousness of heart before God that he honors, as seen in his being "entreated" — answering those who humble themselves in genuine dependence on him.

context: verse 31, the Bible says then we departed from the river of Ahava
point_summary: Earnest prayer and fasting, demonstrated by Ezra's company before their departure, moved God to place His hand upon them and deliver them from their enemies—showing that God answers those who seek Him with humility and seriousness.

context: and the hand of our God was upon us, and he delivered us from the hand of the enemy
point_summary: God's hand of protection and deliverance came upon Ezra's company specifically in answer to their earnest, humbled prayer and fasting, demonstrating that God is entreated by those who seriously seek him.

context: verse 32, and we came to Jerusalem
point_summary: Verse 32's arrival in Jerusalem is cited as the fulfilled destination of a journey undertaken through prayer and fasting, demonstrating that God safely accomplished what He alone could do.
